text: Introduction to RTL, Digital Design & VLSI Context
html: Introduction to RTL, Digital Design & VLSI Context

text: 1. What is Digital Design? (Very First Concept)
html: 1. What is Digital Design? (Very First Concept)

text: In digital design, we design hardware circuits that work using binary values:
html: In digital design, we design hardware circuits that work using binary values:

• Logic 0 → LOW (0V)
  • Logic 1 → HIGH (e.g., 1V / 5V)

• Logic gates
  • Flip-flops
  • Registers
  • Counters
  • State machines

text: All processors, memories, SoCs, and chips are built from these blocks.
html: All processors, memories, SoCs, and chips are built from these blocks.

• Calculator
  • Mobile processor
  • Washing machine controller
  • Traffic light controller

text: All of them internally use digital circuits.
html: All of them internally use digital circuits.

text: 2. What is VLSI and Where RTL Fits?
html: 2. What is VLSI and Where RTL Fits?

text: VLSI means putting millions or billions of transistors on a single chip.
html: VLSI means putting millions or billions of transistors on a single chip.

• Specification
  • RTL Design
  • Functional Verification
  • Logic Synthesis
  • Physical Design (Floorplan, Placement, Routing)
  • Timing Closure
  • Fabrication

text: RTL is the FIRST HARDWARE IMPLEMENTATION STEP
html: RTL is the FIRST HARDWARE IMPLEMENTATION STEP
3. What is RTL (Register Transfer Level)?

text: RTL describes how data moves between registers on a clock edge.
html: RTL describes how data moves between registers on a clock edge.

text: RTL tells what operation happens and when it happens with respect to clock
html: RTL tells what operation happens and when it happens with respect to clock

text: “At every rising edge of clock, add A and B and store the result in register C”
html: “At every rising edge of clock, add A and B and store the result in register C”

text: That sentence itself is RTL thinking.
html: That sentence itself is RTL thinking.

• Register
  • Clock
  • Transfer
  • Combinational logic between registers

text: 4. Why RTL is Needed (Very Important for Students)
html: 4. Why RTL is Needed (Very Important for Students)

text: Why not directly design transistor-level circuits?
html: Why not directly design transistor-level circuits?

• Too complex
  • Time-consuming
  • Error-prone
  • Not reusable

• Abstraction
  • Speed
  • Reusability
  • Technology independence

text: RTL code works for 28nm, 7nm, 5nm without change.
html: RTL code works for 28nm, 7nm, 5nm without change.

• A Hardware Description Language (HDL)
  • Used to describe hardware behavior and structure

content:
Software Language

HDL (Verilog)

Executes line by line

Executes parallel

Describes algorithm

Describes hardware

No clock concept

Clock-based

One CPU

Millions of gates

text: In hardware, both run at the same time.
html: In hardware, both run at the same time.
6. Hardware Thinking vs Software Thinking

• One instruction after another
  • Single execution path

• Everything happens at the same time
  • Clock controls state updates

text: This is the biggest mental shift for students
html: This is the biggest mental shift for students

• Stores 1 or more bits
  • Updates only on clock edge

• D Flip-Flop stores 1 bit
  • Register stores multiple bits

• Output depends ONLY on present input
  • No memory

• Adder
  • MUX
  • Decoder

• Present input
  • Previous output (state)

• Flip-flops
  • Counters
  • FSM

text: 9. RTL Coding Styles (Overview – Will Deep Dive Later)
html: 9. RTL Coding Styles (Overview – Will Deep Dive Later)

text: RTL can be written in three styles:
html: RTL can be written in three styles:
• What the circuit does

text: 10. What You Will Learn in This RTL Course (Assurance)
html: 10. What You Will Learn in This RTL Course (Assurance)

• Write clean synthesizable RTL
  • Understand combinational & sequential circuits
  • Code half adder → FSM
  • Understand why PD engineers care about RTL
  • Avoid common RTL mistakes

• RTL is heart of digital design
  • Verilog is used to describe hardware
  • Hardware ≠ software
  • Everything is clock-driven
  • This foundation is mandatory before PD

text: Verilog Basics | Module Structure | RTL Building Blocks
html: Verilog Basics | Module Structure | RTL Building Blocks

text: Level: Beginner  Goal: Student should confidently write and read basic RTL code
html:
Level: Beginner
  Goal: Student should confidently write and read basic RTL code

text: Verilog is a Hardware Description Language (HDL) used to:
html: Verilog is a Hardware Description Language (HDL) used to:

• Describe digital hardware
  • Model combinational and sequential circuits
  • Simulate and synthesize real silicon hardware

text: Verilog does NOT describe software  Verilog describes how hardware behaves and connects
html:
Verilog does NOT describe software
 Verilog describes how hardware behaves and connects
2. Structure of a Verilog Design

text: Every Verilog design is built using MODULES
html: Every Verilog design is built using MODULES

• Module = Black box
  • Inputs → Processing → Outputs

• module and_gate → Name of the hardware block
  • input a, b → Input pins
  • output y → Output pin
  • assign → Continuous assignment (combinational logic)

• input
  • output
  • inout (used rarely in RTL)

text: Inputs drive the module  Outputs are driven by the module
html:
Inputs drive the module
 Outputs are driven by the module

text: 5. Data Types in Verilog (Critical for Beginners)
html: 5. Data Types in Verilog (Critical for Beginners)

content:
Type

Meaning

wire

Used for combinational connections

reg

Used to store values (inside always block)

html:
• Represents physical wire
  • Cannot store value
  • Used with assign

• Represents storage
  • Used inside always blocks
  • Holds value until changed

text: reg does NOT mean register always  Depends on how it is coded
html:
reg does NOT mean register always
  Depends on how it is coded

text: Executes continuously  Hardware equivalent: logic gates

html:
• Sequential logic
  • Complex combinational logic

text: @(*) → All inputs automatically included  Use blocking assignment (=)
html:
@(*) → All inputs automatically included
 Use blocking assignment (=)

Use non-blocking assignment (<=)
  Represents flip-flop
10. Blocking vs Non-Blocking Assignment (Very Important)

• Used in combinational
  • Executes line by line

• Used in sequential
  • Executes in parallel

• Combinational → =
  • Sequential → <=

text: always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin
html: always @(posedge clk or posedge rst) begin

• Verifies logic correctness
  • Uses testbench
  • No real hardware

• Converts RTL → gates
  • Used for chip fabrication

text: Some code simulates but does NOT synthesize
html: Some code simulates but does NOT synthesize

• Write RTL
  • Compile
  • Simulate
  • Debug
  • Synthesize

text: A counter is a sequential circuit that:

html:
• Counts clock pulses
  • Changes output in a fixed sequence

content:
Type

Description

Up Counter

Counts 0 → 15

Down Counter

Counts 15 → 0

Up-Down Counter

Both directions

Mod-N Counter

Counts 0 → N-1

Ring Counter

Single 1 circulates

Johnson Counter

Twisted ring

html:
• On reset → counter = 0
  • Every clock → increment

• Digital clocks
  • Frequency division

• Only one bit = 1
  • Rotates each clock

• Stores data
  • Shifts left or right

text: Finite State Machines (FSM) – COMPLETE BEGINNER TO INDUSTRY LEVEL
html: Finite State Machines (FSM) – COMPLETE BEGINNER TO INDUSTRY LEVEL

text: A Finite State Machine (FSM) is a sequential circuit that:
html: A Finite State Machine (FSM) is a sequential circuit that:

• Has a finite number of states
  • Changes state based on:

• Present state
  • Input
  • Clock

text: 2. Why FSM is Important in Industry?
html: 2. Why FSM is Important in Industry?

• Traffic light controllers
  • USB controllers
  • Memory controllers
  • Cache controllers
  • Handshake logic
  • Protocols (I2C, SPI, AXI)
• Output depends only on state
• Output depends on state + input

content:
Feature

Moore

Mealy

Output change

On clock

Immediately

Speed

Slower

Faster

Glitches

No

Possible

Industry use

More

Less

text: Moore FSM is preferred in RTL design
html: Moore FSM is preferred in RTL design

text: Every FSM in industry follows these steps:
html: Every FSM in industry follows these steps:

• Problem statement
  • State diagram
  • State table
  • State encoding
  • RTL coding
  • Simulation

• Input = sw
  • Output = led
  • Toggle LED when switch = 1

• State register
  • Next state logic
  • Output logic

text: RTL Coding Guidelines, Simulation vs Synthesis & Timing-Safe Coding
html: RTL Coding Guidelines, Simulation vs Synthesis & Timing-Safe Coding

text: (This class is CRITICAL for interviews + real project success)
html: (This class is CRITICAL for interviews + real project success)

text: 1. Why RTL Coding Guidelines are IMPORTANT?
html: 1. Why RTL Coding Guidelines are IMPORTANT?

text: RTL code is written once, but it is:
html: RTL code is written once, but it is:

• Simulated
  • Synthesized
  • Timed
  • Placed & Routed

text: Bad RTL = Timing failure, latch inference, wrong silicon
html: Bad RTL = Timing failure, latch inference, wrong silicon

text: 2. Simulation vs Synthesis (MOST CONFUSING FOR BEGINNERS)
html: 2. Simulation vs Synthesis (MOST CONFUSING FOR BEGINNERS)

• Software behavior check
  • Uses event-based execution
  • Accepts any logical code

• Converts RTL → Gates
  • Hardware realization
  • Only synthesizable constructs allowed

content:
Feature

Simulation

Synthesis

Purpose

Verify logic

Build hardware

Executes

Sequentially

Parallel hardware

Delays

#10 allowed

 Not allowed

Loops

Any

Must be bounded

html:
• Executes line by line
  • Used for combinational logic

• Executes in parallel
  • Used for sequential logic

• Level sensitive
  • Enable based
  • Unintentional → BAD

• Edge triggered
  • Clock based
  • Preferred

text: 1. What is an FSM (Finite State Machine)?
html: 1. What is an FSM (Finite State Machine)?

text: An FSM is a sequential circuit that:

html:
• Has finite number of states
  • Changes state on clock edge
  • Output depends on:

• Present state
  • Inputs

• State Register (Flip-Flops)
  • Next State Logic
  • Output Logic
  • Clock + Reset
• Output depends only on state
• Output depends on state + input

4. FSM EXAMPLE 1 – SIMPLE TOGGLE FSM
• Output toggles every clock

• S0 → Q=0
  • S1 → Q=1

• Detect input sequence 101
  • Output = 1 when detected

• S0 → Start
  • S1 → Got 1
  • S2 → Got 10

S1: next = in ? S1 : S2;
S2: next = in ? S1 : S0;
